text: 2005:26, Graulich 1989: 63–4). Any safe or convincing conclusion about the origin of and motive behind the volador ritual is difficult to draw. Perhaps Stresser-Peán it right when he says that it is perfectly possible that the Indians attributed several different meanings to the ritual at the same time (Stresser-Peán 2005: 27). Michel Graulich makes this concluding remark: ‘Obviously the ritual always aims at assuring fertility and the voladores mostly represented the dead who came down to impregnate the earth’ (Graulich 1989: 64). And for our purposes this will suffice as a preliminary conclusion. The Indian Charak-puja, or the ‘hook swinging’ ritual The hook-swinging ritual, also known as charak-puja, is a part of the traditional Bengali festival dedicated to Shiva and his wife Durga. Because of written From Liminal to Liminoid to Limivoid 177 sources and direct oral testimonies, we know that the origin of the ritual has its foundations in a myth where child birth and crop fertility are the main concerns. The charak-puja has been practised widely in India since long before the British colonization. One of the earliest reports of the ritual was written in 1582 by Caspero Balbi. The translation of charak-puja would be the ‘wheel form of worship’ or ‘revolving ceremony’ (Powell 1914:148). The agonizing ritual involves a high pole, with an attached frame from which a person’s suspended body hangs in hooks inserted to the skin of the back in a manner that allows rotating in a vertical plane. The pole ritual is still performed today on special occasions. A description of the customary ritual comes from J. Powell who witnessed and photographed the ritual in 1912. Accordingly, the person who performed the ritual had by the means of expert helpers two iron hooks passed through the fleshy portion of the back, one on either side of the spine, just above the kidneys. With a rope attached to the hooks, he was then gently lifted up in the air from a tall platform, and was moved around on a rotating bar that lay across the top of an upright pole, forty feet tall. He was thus rotated from two to five minutes, or stayed suspended as long as he wished. Bells and garlands of flowers were hung around his head and neck during the ceremony. As soon as he was taken down he lay on his stomach for the hooks to be extracted by the men who inserted them (Powell 1914:153). Interestingly, but not surprisingly, the rite was performed only when special rules of behaviour had been observed in due time before the ritual was conducted. Three days before the hook-swinging ceremony took place the participator was in a state of taboo – exactly like neophytes have been documented to be across a variety of world cultures. He was not allowed to eat, and drank water only once a day. He was required to seclude himself from his family and wife, and could speak to no one. As soon as the hooks had been withdrawn after the ceremony he was allowed to drink water sweetened with sugar as the first thing (Powell 1914: 153). It is said that only the lower caste people were ‘swung’, whereas the people belonging to the higher ones, who were already in the gods’ favour, were exempt. With regards to interpretation, we are therefore on relatively safe ground to conclude that the charak-puja was and still is a classical ritual passage including the three steps identified by Arnold van Gennep: separation, liminality and reintegration (with a new or enhanced status that is publicly recognized). The written sources available also evidence that the ritual, at least in its origins, was tied to larger cosmological perceptions of birth and death and also to fertility. The Hinduist mythology that sustains the ritual may be a rewritten version of an already existing ‘script’, much like Christian rituals are known to be Christianized versions of more ancient fertility rites tied to pre-Christian cosmological calendars (it is quite likely, for example, that the caste element was introduced at a later stage). Liminality and the Modern 178 Figure 7.3 Rubbing of Lilla Gerum done by Evers Dietrich around 1960 Source: Tanums Hällristningsmuseum, Sweden. From Liminal to Liminoid to Limivoid 179 The Rock Carvings at Gerum: Bronze Age Pole Jumping? The last of our cases stands out from the rest as it takes the form of a rock carving from Lilla Gerum in Bohuslän. Due to the fact that we have no ethnographic evidence, no written sources or other valid data to support our ideas about this image, interpretations must remain somewhat speculative. The image nevertheless inspires the assumption that what is seen on the rock carving might be some kind of ‘pole-jumping’ ritual. The larger argument we are pursuing here does not stand or fall with the Gerum image; yet, since this is the most ancient known depiction of what might be a possible pole jumping act, any comparative effort must at least make mention of it. And the scene depicted is indeed a striking one. The carving is pecked into a relatively smooth, down sloping rock face, and was made more than 3000 years ago by an unknown prehistoric ‘artist’. The image has been dated to the Bronze Age (1800–500 bc) and it is found in an area with many other rock carvings. 2 The carving is a part of a nine metre by six metre wide stone panel displaying numerous other carved figures such as ships, humans, animals, footprints, circles and cup marks. The image is made with a stone pegging technique, with simple, full drawn lines. Each line has the breadth of a human finger, more or less. The central element in what we assume to be a pole-ritual scene contains a vertical positioned, stylized tree or pole with a platform on top of it. The pole is placed on the deck of a ship (the ship, however, might be a later addition), drawn in a highly stylized manner. An upright human figure
